article.title: Debian Blocks Non-Reproducible Packages From Testing as Forky Cycle Makes Bit-for-Bit Builds Mandatory
article.summary: Debian's release team enabled migration software on May 9 to block packages that cannot be rebuilt byte-for-byte, with 98.29% of Forky's arch-independent packages already reproducible.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Debian's release team has elevated reproducible builds from an aspirational quality goal to a hard migration requirement, with the project's automated tooling now blocking packages from entering the testing archive if they cannot be rebuilt byte-for-byte. The change was announced by release team member Paul Gevers in a "Bits from the release team" message to the debian-devel-announce mailing list on [May 10, 2026](https://lists.debian.org/debian-devel-announce/2026/05/msg00001.html), with enforcement having begun the previous day, according to [It's FOSS News](https://itsfoss.com/news/debian-makes-reproducible-builds-mandatory/).

The policy applies to the in-development Debian 14 release, codenamed "Forky," which The Register places approximately halfway through its release cycle and expects to ship around May 2027, [The Register reported](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094).

## What We Know

In his announcement, Gevers wrote that "Debian must ship reproducible packages," framing the change as a project-wide commitment rather than an opt-in goal, [The Register reported](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094). The same wording appears in Gevers's [original mailing-list message](https://lists.debian.org/debian-devel-announce/2026/05/msg00001.html), which also describes that the release team "enabled migration software to block migration of new packages that can't be reproduced or existing packages (in testing) that regress in reproducibility," per [It's FOSS News](https://itsfoss.com/news/debian-makes-reproducible-builds-mandatory/).

The scope is broader than a green-field rule: packages already in testing that break reproducibility are also blocked from further migrations, [It's FOSS News reported](https://itsfoss.com/news/debian-makes-reproducible-builds-mandatory/). [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/debian-now-blocks-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing/) similarly notes that the change applies retroactively to packages already in testing.

A reproducible build, as [heise online explains](https://www.heise.de/en/news/Debian-14-Reproducible-builds-become-mandatory-11289352.html), generates "bit-identical binary packages from identical source code and in the same build environment." Verification relies on `.buildinfo` files captured during the original build, which record the exact toolchain and environment so a second party can attempt the same compilation and compare results, per [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/debian-now-blocks-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing/). Two long-running tools sit at the heart of the workflow: `rebuilderd`, which continuously rebuilds packages from the archive, and `diffoscope`, which compares binaries to surface non-deterministic differences, according to [PBX Science](https://pbxscience.com/debian-mandates-reproducible-packages-for-debian-14-forky/). Typical culprits — embedded timestamps, random build IDs, and non-deterministic file ordering — are listed in [heise's coverage](https://www.heise.de/en/news/Debian-14-Reproducible-builds-become-mandatory-11289352.html).

At the time of the announcement, 98.29% of architecture-independent packages in Forky reproduced successfully, with 23,731 passing and 414 still flagged as not reproducible, according to [It's FOSS News](https://itsfoss.com/news/debian-makes-reproducible-builds-mandatory/). Current status is tracked on the project's dashboard at reproduce.debian.net, [Linuxiac reported](https://linuxiac.com/debian-now-blocks-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing/).

The release team also enabled autopkgtests for binary-only non-maintainer uploads, known as binNMUs, in the same announcement, putting them on the same testing footing as full source uploads, per the [debian-devel-announce message](https://lists.debian.org/debian-devel-announce/2026/05/msg00001.html). [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/debian-now-blocks-non-reproducible-packages-from-testing/) describes the same change in matching terms. And separately, the recent addition of a new architecture, `loong64`, has triggered widespread package rebuilds and elongated migration queues across the archive, [heise online noted](https://www.heise.de/en/news/Debian-14-Reproducible-builds-become-mandatory-11289352.html).

## Context

The Reproducible Builds project, which has tracked deterministic-build progress across multiple distributions, traces its organized effort back to 2014, [PBX Science reported](https://pbxscience.com/debian-mandates-reproducible-packages-for-debian-14-forky/). Debian's own reproducibility work has been visible publicly for years; The Register notes it first covered the effort in February 2015, [The Register reported](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094). Similar efforts are underway in FreeBSD 15 and in NixOS, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094).

Debian 13, the current stable release, shipped in August 2025, [The Register reported](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094). With Forky still in development, Debian developers have roughly a year to drive the 414 non-reproducible packages toward zero before the release is frozen.

## What We Don't Know

The announcement does not specify how reproducibility regressions in widely depended-on packages will interact with release-critical bug triage in practice, beyond Gevers's reminder that maintainers remain responsible for ensuring their uploads can migrate and should file RC-severity bugs when autopkgtest regressions block dependents, [heise reported](https://www.heise.de/en/news/Debian-14-Reproducible-builds-become-mandatory-11289352.html). It also does not state how the remaining gap to 100% will be closed for the 414 packages that still fail rebuild checks, or whether any class of package will be granted exceptions.

The scope of reproducibility is bounded by Debian's own build environment rather than the broader notion sometimes used by the Reproducible Builds project, a caveat that contributor Gioele Barabucci flagged in follow-up discussion, [The Register reported](https://www.theregister.com/oses/2026/05/11/debian-14-cracks-down-on-unreproducible-packages/5238094) — meaning third parties rebuilding outside Debian's infrastructure may still see differences that the project's own tooling will not flag.
